name: Irish Soda Bread
recipeInstructions:
Calories per serving: 154 Approx. Cook Time: 1:00
"Sour milk" is milk that has had a couple of teaspoons of buttermilk stirred into it, then left in a warm place for about 24 hours. The original Irish name is bainne clabhair, or "clabbered milk". The flavor isn't quite as tart as buttermilk, but there's enough acid to make the baking soda react correctly. If you don't have sour milk, buttermilk will work fine. Sweet milk won't work as well; use baking powder instead if necessary.
First, decide whether you're making farl or cake. If farl, preheat a heavy frying pan (cast iron is best) to low-medium heat. If making cake, preheat the oven to 450°F and prepare a baking sheet. Preheating is important for soda bread.
Sift together the dry ingredients several times to ensure even distribution of the baking soda. Make a well in the center of the dry mixture and add about one cup of sour milk. Stir until you have a raggy, soft dough that looks slightly floury but is very squishy when poked. Add more liquid sparingly if needed.
Transfer the dough to a lightly floured board and knead quickly using your hands. Don't overmix; avoid developing gluten for a tough loaf. Knead until the dough forms one cohesive lump.
For cake, shape the dough into a circle about 6-8 inches in diameter on the prepared baking sheet. Cut a cross through the center with a sharp knife (the cuts should be about halfway through). For farl, roll the dough into a thick circle that will yield loaves about 3/4 inch thick when cut. Dust the pan or griddle with flour before adding the farls to prevent sticking.
Bake for 45 minutes at 450°F. Check by tapping the bottom of the cake; it should sound hollow when done. For a crunchy crust, let cool on a rack. For a softer crust, wrap the cake in a clean dishcloth immediately after removing from the oven.
